# Conclusion

Rigid pavements have been the quiet exception in a decade of machine-learning progress on IRI prediction —
not because they are unimportant, but because no one had built the pipeline to include them on equal footing
with asphalt. We built that pipeline directly against FHWA's public LTPP distribution, fixed three real
data-engineering bugs along the way (pavement-family misclassification, an overly tight survey-matching
window, and section-level train/test leakage), added the FWD load-transfer-efficiency feature that a first
draft of this work had only flagged as future work, replaced ad hoc hyperparameters with an Optuna search
against group-aware cross-validation, replaced traffic-variable exclusion with leakage-safe imputation and an
explicit ablation, and tested — rather than assumed — whether JPCP/JRCP and CRCP should share a model. The
resulting structural models recover known pavement-engineering mechanisms from data alone and outperform
mechanistic-empirical benchmarks substantially; a companion operational variant with lagged IRI reaches
$R^2$ up to 0.70 for agencies whose priority is forecasting rather than mechanism attribution. We report every
place tuning did *not* help (pooled-rigid hyperparameter search, the ANN baseline, JPCP/JRCP-only splitting)
with the same prominence as the places it did, because a Q1-caliber contribution should be judged on whether
its claims survive scrutiny, not on how favorably its numbers can be arranged. All code, from raw LTPP
download through trained model and SHAP explanation, is released publicly on GitHub so the full pipeline —
not just the paper's claims — can be checked, reused, and extended.

## Data and Code Availability

The complete reproducible pipeline (data acquisition, cleaning, modeling, and this manuscript) is available on
GitHub at `https://github.com/sabernaseralavi-60/2026_International-Roughness-Index-Prediction-Using-the-LTPP`
under an open license. Raw LTPP data is public domain, distributed by FHWA InfoPave.
